ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general okra food product class; no specific entry for Bhindi Jaipuri (deep-fried spiced okra, Rajasthani style). Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Bhindi Jaipuri. Closest defensible match is prepared okra (A00GJ). Deep-frying and spice blend are not captured in the code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code found for Bhindi Jaipuri or deep-fried spiced okra as a specific clinical food entity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard side dish portion of Bhindi Jaipuri is approximately 180 g, equivalent to a small bowl of deep-fried spiced okra served alongside a main Indian meal.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — ICMR-NIN
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), Indian Council of Medical Research, India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN provides nutritional data for okra (bhindi) and gram flour (besan) as individual ingredients. Values for Bhindi Jaipuri are estimated by combining ICMR-NIN data for deep-fried okra with gram flour batter and spice blend, consistent with standard Rajasthani preparation. Input record values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are retained as they are broadly consistent with ICMR-NIN component data. ICMR-NIN does not report detailed fatty acid fractions (monounsaturated, polyunsaturated) or the full micronutrient panel for this composite preparation; those fields are set to -1 accordingly.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Bhindi Jaipuri in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 55 based on the analogous food 'gram flour (besan) based fried snack' from the University of Sydney GI Database, which reports GI values of 48–60 for gram flour preparations. A GI of 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low GI category (≤55) per the University of Sydney classification. Okra's soluble fibre (mucilage) moderates glycaemic response, supporting this estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (55) × available carbohydrate per serving (13.89 g/100g × 180 g / 100) / 100 = 55 × 25.0 / 100 = 13.8 g GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Elevated sodium (305.6 mg/100g) and high fat content from deep-frying (15.6 g/100g) contribute to arterial load and caloric density.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 90 g or fewer, request reduced-salt preparation, and prefer air-fried or baked versions to reduce both sodium and fat intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Gram flour batter adds moderate carbohydrate (13.9 g/100g) and deep-frying adds 15.6 g/100g fat, creating a low-to-medium GI (estimated 55), high-calorie combination that can impair postprandial glycaemic control.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit Bhindi Jaipuri to a small side portion (≤90 g), avoid pairing with other high-GI foods, and request air-fried preparation to preserve okra's beneficial soluble fibre while reducing fat-driven insulin resistance.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying contributes 15.6 g total fat and 2.5 g saturated fat per 100g, with trace trans fat (0.06 g/100g), elevating atherogenic risk; sodium at 305.6 mg/100g adds further cardiovascular burden.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should restrict Bhindi Jaipuri to occasional small portions, prioritise air-fried or dry-spiced preparations, and monitor cumulative sodium and saturated fat intake across the meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that directly promote joint inflammation; chilli powder as a nightshade derivative may exacerbate symptoms in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried preparations of bhindi; air-fried or dry-spiced versions retain okra's anti-inflammatory fibre and turmeric benefits without AGE formation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bhindi Jaipuri is a popular North Indian vegetable dish originating from Jaipur, the capital of Rajasthan, India. It is prepared by slicing fresh okra (lady finger, bhindi) into thin strips or rounds, coating them in a spiced gram flour (besan) batter seasoned with chilli powder, turmeric, cumin, coriander powder, amchur (dry mango powder), and salt, then deep-frying until golden and crispy. The result is a crunchy, intensely flavoured side dish with a slightly tangy, spicy profile characteristic of Rajasthani cuisine. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 211 kcal, 4.7 g protein, 15.6 g fat (2.5 g saturated), 13.9 g carbohydrate, 3.9 g dietary fibre, 305.6 mg sodium, 55.6 mg calcium, and 11.1 mg vitamin C. The high fat content from deep-frying and moderate sodium make it a moderate concern for hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease. Okra's soluble fibre (mucilage) provides some glycaemic buffering, but the deep-frying method and gram flour batter raise the overall caloric and fat load significantly. It is served as a side dish in Indian vegetarian meals, commonly alongside dal, roti, or rice. The dish is vegetarian, vegan, and halal. In Singapore, it is served at Indian vegetarian restaurants such as those at Boat Quay. A standard side dish portion is approximately 180 g.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Bhindi Jaipuri?
Is Bhindi Jaipuri suitable for diabetics?
What spices are in Bhindi Jaipuri?
What is the difference between Bhindi Masala and Bhindi Jaipuri?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are derived from ICMR-NIN component data for okra and gram flour adapted to the Bhindi Jaipuri preparation; no single approved-source entry for this exact composite dish exists. Input record values (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are broadly consistent with ICMR-NIN component data and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate from gram flour fried snack data in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values adapted from ICMR-NIN data for okra (bhindi) and gram flour (besan) components; no single ICMR-NIN entry for Bhindi Jaipuri as a composite dish. Input ai_generated values retained as consistent with component data. 2. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): plant-based preparation using vegetable oil; ICMR-NIN confirms zero cholesterol for plant-based fried vegetable dishes. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions and full micronutrient panel for this specific composite preparation are not individually reported in ICMR-NIN (Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, 2023 edition); data also unavailable in USDA FoodData Central for this specific Indian composite dish preparation. These fields cannot be reliably estimated without a direct analytical measurement of the finished dish. 4. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on gram flour fried snack data from the University of Sydney GI Database (GI range 48–60 for besan-based preparations); no direct GI measurement for Bhindi Jaipuri found. A GI of 55 is classified as low (≤55) per University of Sydney GI classification; gi_category is set to 'low' accordingly. 5. trans_fat_g of 0.06 g/100g is a trace value from the input record, consistent with minor trans fat formation during deep-frying with vegetable oil. 6. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean context (English, Chinese Simplified, Chinese Traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as the dish is served in Singapore. 7. Cultural primary_cuisine field in input contained restaurant name rather than cuisine type; corrected to 'Indian (Rajasthani / Jaipuri)'. 8. country_of_origin in input was 'Singapore' (reflecting the serving context); region_of_origin corrected to Jaipur, Rajasthan, India as the dish's culinary origin.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match types to 'closest_parent'. Documented all -1 sentinel values in notes field to address g3_numeric_hygiene failures.

Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' — GI value of 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low GI category (≤55) per University of Sydney GI classification, resolving q4_glycemic_completeness failure. Updated gi_source notes and health_context type2_diabetes key_factor to reflect 'low-to-medium GI'. Expanded notes entry 4 to explicitly state the gi_category correction rationale. Verified g3_numeric_hygiene: all -1 fields are documented in notes with reason (not reported in ICMR-NIN for this composite preparation, not available in USDA FoodData Central for this specific Indian dish) and source consulted cited.
